_meta.dna: narrative(product) = story(true) x angle(surprising) x proof(undeniable) x timing(now)
_meta.question_expansion_instruction: Each floor lists 7 seed questions. During execution, expand to 100 questions per persona by probing deeper into the domain. Focus on whether a journalist could verify claims independently, whether media assets exist, and whether the narrative survives hostile questioning.

name: Tower of Press
protocol: FORECAST -> CROSS-REF -> AUDIT -> FIX -> VERIFY -> SEAL -> POSTMORTEM

questions: Is the architecture novel enough to warrant a deep-dive article?
Can a technically sophisticated journalist verify all architecture claims independently from the codebase?
Does the PBKDF2 iteration count in code match the security SOP (600K vs actual)?
Are SHA-256 hash chains actually tamper-evident as claimed, or just tamper-detectable?
Is the recipe replay zero-cost claim mathematically derivable from the codebase?
Does the local-first execution claim hold under inspection -- are there hidden cloud dependencies?
Would the architecture survive peer review from a distributed systems expert?
Is the token cannibalization moat a genuine strategic insight or a marketing claim?
Can OpenAI or Anthropic build recipe replay without cannibalizing their own revenue?
Are the unit economics ($8/month Starter, $5.75/user COGS) mathematically defensible?
Does the FSL license create a meaningful competitive differentiation versus MIT/Apache?
Is there a credible path from zero revenue to sustainable business?
Does the strategy account for Google, Microsoft, and Apple building native automation?
Would Ben Thompson identify this as genuine disruption or incremental improvement?
Is there a compelling human story behind the product's creation?
Are there user testimonials that prove real-world impact beyond simulated personas?
Does the Bruce Lee brand philosophy resonate as a cultural story or feel forced?
Is there a vulnerable, honest moment in the founder story that creates connection?
Would a Wired reader care about this product without a technical background?
Does the Dragon Rider philosophy translate into a readable narrative arc?
Is the evidence-first philosophy connected to broader trust-in-AI cultural concerns?
Is there a consumer-facing product that a Verge reviewer could actually install and test?
Is a binary installer available (AppImage, DMG, MSI) or does it require pip install?
Does the onboarding flow work in under 5 minutes for a non-developer?
Is the YinYang approval dialog intuitive without reading documentation?
Does the UI pass a basic aesthetics review for a consumer tech audience?
Is there a mobile app or mobile-accessible interface?
Would a non-technical reviewer describe the value proposition in one sentence?
Is there a newsworthy event (launch, funding, partnership, milestone) to report?
Are user metrics available (signups, DAU, revenue) for the article?
Does the product address a problem that VCs are actively funding competitors for?
Is the founding story compelling for a startup media audience?
Does the bootstrapped counter-narrative differentiate or just signal lack of traction?
Is the beta launch date framed as a news event with a press release?
Can TechCrunch find a named source (investor, customer, advisor) to quote?
Is there a compelling EU privacy angle (GDPR-aware, local-first, no telemetry)?
Does the FSL license differentiate in the European open-source ecosystem?
Is there a European user base, office, or partnership to ground the EU story?
Does the product have GDPR certification or Data Protection Impact Assessment?
Are there EU-specific compliance claims that can be independently verified?
Does the product address EU AI Act requirements for transparency and evidence?
Would TNW's European audience find this more relevant than a US-only tool?
Does the OAuth3 governance story matter to CIOs and enterprise policy makers?
Are there enterprise customers, pilots, or design partners to reference?
Has the OAuth3 spec been submitted to any standards body (IETF, W3C)?
Is there SOC2, ISO 27001, or any compliance certification?
Does the product have enterprise pricing with SSO, SLA, and audit features?
Would a Fortune 500 CIO consider this for a pilot based on current documentation?
Is there a policy engagement story (regulatory meetings, standards participation)?
Is there a contrarian take that Information subscribers would pay to read?
Is there insider intelligence about token vendor strategy that supports the moat thesis?
Does the token cannibalization analysis hold under The Information's analytical rigor?
Is there executive drama, competitive intelligence, or strategic pivot to report?
Would The Information's audience (VCs, operators) find actionable insight here?
Is there a scoop that no other outlet has covered yet?
Does the analysis reveal something non-obvious about the AI agent market structure?

Would a financial journalist with a Bloomberg terminal score my narrative 7/7, not just a philosophy podcast host?

floors.persona_domain: ultimate press readiness -- narrative survival final judgment
questions: Can the product's narrative survive a front-page story written by the most hostile journalist in the room?
Is there a single sentence that every journalist -- from Ars Technica to Bloomberg -- would repeat accurately?
Are all CRITICAL and HIGH gaps from floors 1-47 resolved before this product speaks to any press?
Is the Architecture-Business gap acknowledged honestly in every press interaction, or is it obscured?
Does the product have enough proof points to support the narrative without asking the journalist to take anything on faith?
Is there a communications strategy that addresses every adversarial persona (floors 41-47) before launch?
Final verdict: is this product ready for press -- YES or NO, with one honest sentence of justification?
